title: Kaboom

jokes.text: $20 to Netflix. $20 to Blockbuster Online. Both?
jokes.setup: Credit card company lists streaming services as suspicious charges
jokes.punchline: April has subscriptions to competing services
jokes.explanation: Simple observational humor about redundant subscriptions - relatable but not particularly clever

dialogue.speaker: Credit Card Representative
dialogue.line: We've flagged some suspicious charges on your account. Multiple streaming services in a single month?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Those aren't suspicious. I have Netflix, Hulu, Disney+, HBO Max, and Amazon Prime.

dialogue.speaker: Credit Card Representative
dialogue.line: Ma'am, that's five different services. Most people just pick one.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Most people are sheep. I like to keep my options open so I can hate all of them equally.

jokes.text: I needed all 11 discs of gossip girl at the same time.
jokes.setup: Explanation for having both Netflix and Blockbuster
jokes.punchline: Nonsensical reason - needing all discs simultaneously
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - April's deadpan delivery of absurd logic fits her personality

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I have Netflix and Blockbuster because sometimes I need to watch all the discs at the same time.

jokes.text: $120 in tuition to Hogwarts School of Witchcraft and Wizardry. They give you a little wand and a diploma, it's fun.
jokes.setup: Another suspicious charge is read
jokes.punchline: April matter-of-factly explains paying for fake Hogwarts tuition
jokes.explanation: Perfect April character beat - her earnest explanation of obviously fake purchases

dialogue.speaker: Credit Card Representative
dialogue.line: There's a charge here for Hogwarts School of Witchcraft and Wizardry for $3,500.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Yeah, that's my tuition.

dialogue.speaker: Credit Card Representative
dialogue.line: Ma'am, Hogwarts isn't real.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I know that. But the tuition is.

jokes.text: What'd you major in? Potions.
jokes.setup: Follow-up question about the fake Hogwarts education
jokes.punchline: April continues the bit with deadpan specificity
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation - the deadpan 'Potions' answer shows April's commitment to the absurd premise

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I got a 3.8 GPA at Hogwarts. I was in Ravenclaw. I wrote my thesis on the mating habits of hippogriffs.

jokes.text: Man-Pillow, the pillow shaped like a man. Also something called 'bucket of cake'.
jokes.setup: More suspicious purchases being read
jokes.punchline: Increasingly ridiculous product names
jokes.explanation: Solid absurdist humor - the product names are just ridiculous enough to be funny

dialogue.speaker: Credit Card Representative
dialogue.line: We've also got charges for Butt Pucker Lip Gloss, Booty Sweat Energy Drink, and a product called Colon Blow Cereal.

jokes.text: You know what? I think someone stole my credit card so cancel it. Don't refund anything.
jokes.setup: April realizes she's revealed too much about her purchases
jokes.punchline: She tries to end the call but reveals she actually wants to keep the items
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing April's self-awareness and desire to keep her ridiculous purchases

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Wait, no, I didn't mean— okay, fine, yes, I bought them. But I'm hanging up now.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: And don't you dare tell anyone I'm keeping them. Especially not Jerry.

jokes.text: So... What does a man-pillow look like? Daniel Craig. It's for my lower back.
jokes.setup: Someone asks about the man-pillow April bought
jokes.punchline: April reveals it looks like Daniel Craig and claims it's for back support
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback with good escalation - the Daniel Craig detail and fake medical excuse are perfectly April

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: It's Daniel Craig. For back support.

jokes.text: High five. I never trust anything that quickly. That's why I don't eat minute rice.
jokes.setup: Enthusiastic Kaboom volunteer wants a high five
jokes.punchline: Ron's suspicious nature extends to minute rice
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron Swanson moment - his paranoid worldview applied to mundane things like rice preparation

dialogue.speaker: Keith
dialogue.line: High five!

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I don't trust it. It's too fast.

jokes.text: Fat kids, skinny kids, brainiacs, sluts, the gay drama kids, goths, jocks, the alternative crowd.
jokes.setup: Leslie talks about all the kids who will use the playground
jokes.punchline: She casually includes 'sluts' in her wholesome list
jokes.explanation: Good escalation joke - Leslie's earnest inclusivity accidentally includes inappropriate category

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: This playground is going to be used by all kinds of kids. Jocks, nerds, preps, goths, sluts...

jokes.text: The key to volunteering? A lot of pockets. For putting all the food in. The red cross has amazing cookies.
jokes.setup: Tom shares his volunteering philosophy
jokes.punchline: He only volunteers for the free food
jokes.explanation: Good Tom character moment - his selfish approach to seemingly altruistic activities

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: He only volunteers for the free food

jokes.text: Suicide hotline, surprisingly lame spread.
jokes.setup: Tom continues listing volunteer opportunities he's exploited
jokes.punchline: He criticizes the catering at a suicide prevention service
jokes.explanation: Dark humor executed well - Tom's shallow complaint about refreshments at a suicide hotline is perfectly inappropriate

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I volunteered at a suicide prevention hotline. The catering was terrible. I mean, if you're already thinking about ending it all, the last thing you need is a sad chicken salad sandwich.

jokes.text: You live in a pit. Not anymore. Living with the drummer of my band. Living indoors. Pretty cool. Not to brag. Kinda hard not to.
jokes.setup: April points out Andy lives in a pit
jokes.punchline: Andy brags about the basic accomplishment of living indoors
jokes.explanation: Good Andy moment - his low standards and pride in basic human achievements

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: You live in a pit.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I live indoors. That's something.

jokes.text: Me and an old Asian lady are double-teaming some monkey bars, so.
jokes.setup: Andy explains he has to go help with playground construction
jokes.punchline: Innocent activity described with unintentionally sexual language
jokes.explanation: Decent innuendo joke - Andy's oblivious word choice creates double meaning

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I have to go help with playground construction.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: That sounds like fun.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Yeah, we're gonna be working with long, hard wood all day. Really getting our hands dirty. Lots of hard thrusting motions.

jokes.text: I got a remote control and I'm controlling you. Go faster! Go faster, Ron's the master.
jokes.setup: Ron finds a remote control at the construction site
jokes.punchline: He pretends to control people with it and makes up a rhyme
jokes.explanation: Silly physical comedy - Ron's rare moment of childlike playfulness

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I found this remote control at the construction site. Let me try something.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Points remote at Jerry. You there, freeze!

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Now dance. Dance for me, puppet man. I control the remote, you control the dance. It rhymes, it's science.

jokes.text: Jerry, did you use permanent marker again? I'm sorry, guys... Forget it. Let's all pretend Jerry wasn't born.
jokes.setup: Jerry has made another mistake with office supplies
jokes.punchline: Leslie's disproportionately harsh response to minor error
jokes.explanation: Standard Jerry abuse joke - fits the show's pattern but not particularly creative

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Jerry, you had ONE job. One job! Do you know how many people work in this office? Twelve. Twelve people, and somehow you managed to mess up the one thing that affects all of us. The copy machine. THE COPY MACHINE, Jerry!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, I just pressed the wrong button—

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Wrong button? WRONG BUTTON?! Do you understand the butterfly effect, Jerry? One small action can have massive consequences. You pressed that button, and now we live in a world where nobody can copy anything. Nobody. That's not a world I want to live in, Jerry. That's not the world our forefathers died for.

jokes.text: I have a couple in my wallet. That's what I call condoms. Come on, Tom. Focus.
jokes.setup: Leslie asks everyone to take out their 'doing caps'
jokes.punchline: Tom reveals he calls condoms 'doing caps'
jokes.explanation: Good Tom character moment - his inappropriate interjection and Leslie's immediate shutdown

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Everyone, take out your doing caps.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Oh, I thought you meant condoms.

jokes.text: I wanna move like a cheetah. Or a slug driving a remote controlled car. Something more plausible than that, but fast.
jokes.setup: Leslie wants to move faster than a slug
jokes.punchline: Her alternative metaphors get increasingly absurd
jokes.explanation: Good Leslie moment - her over-enthusiastic metaphors that spiral into nonsense

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I need to move faster than a slug. Actually, let me rephrase that. I need to move faster than a slug on quaaludes. No wait—faster than a slug on quaaludes riding a very apathetic sloth. Hmm, or a slug on quaaludes riding a sloth that's also on quaaludes, but the sloth took twice as much because it's bigger. Actually, a slug on quaaludes riding a sloth on quaaludes while both are underwater in a vat of molasses during a solar eclipse.

jokes.text: Would you break the rules? I won't murder.
jokes.setup: Mark asks if Leslie would break rules to get things done
jokes.punchline: Leslie immediately jumps to the extreme of murder
jokes.explanation: Good escalation - Leslie's mind immediately going to the most extreme rule-breaking

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: Would you break the rules to get things done?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh my God, yes. I would kill someone.

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: I meant like, bend the rules.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh. No, of course not.

jokes.text: So who gives me the go ahead to not ask for permission, because... Is it Ron?
jokes.setup: Mark tells Leslie to be bold and not ask permission
jokes.punchline: Leslie immediately asks for permission to not ask permission
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy - perfectly captures Leslie's bureaucratic mindset and need for approval

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: You should be bold. Don't ask permission.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Can I ask you something? Do I have your permission to not ask permission?

jokes.text: I've never rented a guy before. Wish I knew about that during prom.
jokes.setup: Leslie talks about renting equipment that comes with an operator
jokes.punchline: Innocent comment about renting people sounds like escort services
jokes.explanation: Basic innuendo joke - Leslie's innocent comment has unintended implications

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: We're going to rent some equipment that comes with an operator.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: So you're renting people?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: That sounds like an escort service.

jokes.text: There was a girl at my prom who was known as the backhoe. Mary Dunbar, she'd let anyone massage her back.
jokes.setup: Tom responds to Leslie's prom comment
jokes.punchline: Expected sexual story turns innocent - 'backhoe' just meant back massages
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection - sets up expectation of sexual story then subverts with innocent explanation

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Oh, you want to know about my prom night? Well, let me tell you, it involved a backhoe.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: A backhoe?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Yeah, my date's dad had one. We went to the construction site, and she gave me a backhoe all night long.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Tom...

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Back massages. She just really liked giving back massages. Her hands were magic.

jokes.text: I didn't wanna tell him my real name, you know?
jokes.setup: Construction worker calls Leslie 'Ms. Montegue'
jokes.punchline: Leslie gave a fake name for simple construction work
jokes.explanation: Decent character moment - Leslie's unnecessary paranoia about routine transactions

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I panicked! I was worried he'd recognize me from the city council and ask me questions about zoning permits, so I just blurted out the first name that came to my head.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Which was?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ms. Montegue.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Where did that come from?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I have no idea.

jokes.text: We are about to fill this hole now. Not with dirt, but with the courage of a thousand lions. And the solemn memory of all of our friends who have fallen in this cursed hole.
jokes.setup: Leslie gives a speech before filling the pit
jokes.punchline: Overly dramatic language for mundane construction work
jokes.explanation: Good Leslie character moment - her tendency to over-dramatize simple government tasks

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: This pit has been a blight on our community for far too long. But today, we rise up. Today, we take back what is ours. Today, we fill this pit.

jokes.text: The monitor is not a toy, Andy. Anything is a toy if you play with it.
jokes.setup: Nurse scolds Andy for playing with medical equipment
jokes.punchline: Andy's childlike philosophy about toys
jokes.explanation: Good Andy moment - his innocent, childlike logic applied to serious situations

dialogue.speaker: Nurse
dialogue.line: You can't just play with the medical equipment!

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: But anything's a toy if you're brave enough.

jokes.text: I saw an old cereal box I hadn't read before, so I must've drifted off.
jokes.setup: Andy explains why he was sleeping in the pit
jokes.punchline: He was reading cereal boxes for entertainment
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Andy's simple pleasures and low standards for entertainment

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I was reading cereal boxes for entertainment.

jokes.text: Can I smoke in here? You don't smoke. Just asking if I can.
jokes.setup: Leslie asks if she can smoke in Ron's office
jokes.punchline: She doesn't even smoke, just curious about permissions
jokes.explanation: Good character beat - Leslie's bureaucratic mind fixated on rules even when irrelevant

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Can I smoke in your office?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What if I was already smoking when I came in?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Then I would ask you to leave.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What if I was smoking a cigar, like you?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I would still ask you to leave.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I don't even smoke. I was just curious about the rules.

jokes.text: Are you high? I'm high on kaboom!
jokes.setup: Ron asks if Leslie is on drugs due to her behavior
jokes.punchline: Leslie's earnest response using the volunteer organization's name
jokes.explanation: Basic wordplay - Leslie's enthusiasm channeled through the organization's branding

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Are you on drugs?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: No, Ron. I'm just on the Pawnee Parks and Recreation Department.

jokes.text: I'm sorry to burst your ka-bubble, but I just had my ass ka-handed to me by the city manager, and now this entire department is ka-screwed.
jokes.setup: Ron needs to discipline Leslie for the pit incident
jokes.punchline: He sarcastically uses 'ka-' prefix for everything
jokes.explanation: Good callback - Ron's dry mockery of Leslie's enthusiasm by over-using the organization's prefix

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Leslie, I'm going to have to write you up for the pit incident. Ka-incident.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: This is a ka-serious violation of department policy.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: You'll be receiving a ka-formal reprimand that will go in your ka-permanent file.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: This is no ka-joke, Leslie.

jokes.text: What the ka-*** were you thinking?
jokes.setup: Ron continues his sarcastic use of 'ka-' prefix
jokes.punchline: Uses it with profanity to emphasize his anger
jokes.explanation: Decent escalation of the ka- prefix joke with profanity for emphasis

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Ka-blam! Ka-pow! Ka-chow! You know what? Ka-SHUT THE F*** UP!

jokes.text: I would prefer that she ask me for my permission so I can say no. I like saying no. It lowers their enthusiasm.
jokes.setup: Ron talks about his management philosophy
jokes.punchline: He admits to enjoying crushing people's enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: Excellent Ron character moment - perfectly captures his misanthropic management style and disdain for government enthusiasm

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: I enjoy crushing people's enthusiasm. It's like a morning ritual for me.

jokes.text: My God, my sexy sweater! It has a hole cut out over the bicep.
jokes.setup: Andy sees clothes Tom brought him
jokes.punchline: His idea of sexy fashion is a bicep hole in a sweater
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Andy's misguided fashion sense and what he considers attractive

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: His idea of sexy fashion is a bicep hole in a sweater

jokes.text: A tragic accident reignited feelings you thought were long dead and we are finally back together.
jokes.setup: Andy explains what he thinks is happening with Ann
jokes.punchline: He's created a romantic narrative from a construction accident
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment - Andy's delusion about rekindling romance through injury

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Ann was in a car accident and she's in the hospital. But here's the thing—I think this might be the universe telling us we're meant to be together. I mean, what are the odds? She crashes her car right when I'm finally ready to commit. It's like fate.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: So I've decided I'm going to be there for her every single day. Bring her flowers, sit by her bed, read to her. She won't be able to resist me. This is actually the best thing that could've happened to us.

jokes.text: What does he have that I don't have? Are you serious? Everything.
jokes.setup: Andy asks what Mark has that he doesn't
jokes.punchline: Ann's blunt, comprehensive dismissal
jokes.explanation: Solid relationship comedy - Ann's brutal honesty about Andy's shortcomings

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: What does Mark have that I don't have?

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: A job.

jokes.text: He has literally everything that you don't have: a job, a car, a steady income, an address, a second pair of shoes, table manners, the ability to say 'tampon' without giggling.
jokes.setup: Ann explains what Mark has that Andy doesn't
jokes.punchline: List escalates from serious (job, income) to absurd (tampon giggling)
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation - starts with serious adult responsibilities and ends with childish behavior, perfectly capturing Andy's immaturity

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Mark has a job, a good income, a apartment, a car, maturity, and he doesn't giggle when someone says tampon.

jokes.text: But sometimes when you make an omelet, you gotta break a few eggs. What's the alternative, no omelets at all? Who wants to live in that kind of world? Maybe birds. Then all their babies would live.
jokes.setup: Leslie defends her actions with an omelet metaphor
jokes.punchline: Her logic spirals into thinking about bird welfare
jokes.explanation: Good Leslie moment - her tendency to over-think metaphors and consider unintended consequences

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You can't make an omelet without breaking a few eggs. And yes, I broke some eggs today, but those eggs had it coming.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Wait, do the eggs know they had it coming? Are we asking the eggs first? Because I feel like we should be asking the eggs.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh my God, what about the chickens? Has anyone checked on the chickens?

jokes.text: What I do know is that he could sue us at the drop of a hat. I mean, right now he is the most dangerous man in Pawnee.
jokes.setup: City attorney explains the legal situation
jokes.punchline: Harmless Andy described as most dangerous man in town
jokes.explanation: Decent irony - describing bumbling Andy as a dangerous threat due to legal liability

dialogue.speaker: City Attorney
dialogue.line: I'm afraid we have a serious legal situation here. Andy Dwyer has been classified as the most dangerous man in town.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What? Andy? That's ridiculous. He's harmless!

dialogue.speaker: City Attorney
dialogue.line: According to the statute, anyone who has caused this much chaos unintentionally is deemed a threat to public safety.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait, I'm dangerous? That's awesome!

jokes.text: You can't say 'I'm sorry', or 'I apologize.' It implies guilt. That's insane, I have to apologize. Andy was the victim... Can't say 'victim'.
jokes.setup: Lawyer explains what words Leslie can't say
jokes.punchline: Every normal word is legally problematic
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor about legal restrictions on normal human interaction

dialogue.speaker: Lawyer
dialogue.line: You can't say 'binder' because of the controversy...

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What about 'folder'?

dialogue.speaker: Lawyer
dialogue.line: Also problematic.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Um... 'file'?

dialogue.speaker: Lawyer
dialogue.line: Nope.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: How about just... 'thing'?

dialogue.speaker: Lawyer
dialogue.line: Especially 'thing'. That's the worst one.

jokes.text: I can't say the word 'situation'? No, it implies there was a situation.
jokes.setup: Leslie discovers she can't say basic words
jokes.punchline: The word 'situation' implies a situation exists
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist logic - the circular reasoning of legal language restrictions

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: The word 'situation' implies a situation exists.

jokes.text: Can I give him the pig? Pig's fine.
jokes.setup: Leslie asks what she can safely do
jokes.punchline: Of all things, giving a pig is legally safe
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist moment - random pig gift is somehow legally acceptable when words aren't

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What can I safely do?

dialogue.speaker: Scott
dialogue.line: Of all things, giving a pig is legally safe.

jokes.text: Hope you squeal better.
jokes.setup: Leslie gives Andy a pig stuffed animal
jokes.punchline: Awkward pig pun while trying to be legally careful
jokes.explanation: Weak pun but fits Leslie's awkwardness under legal constraints

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I got you a little something. It's a pig stuffed animal.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Oh, thanks Leslie!

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You could say this gift is... pretty un-be-lievable. Wait, no. You could say it's... sow-some. Actually, I need to be careful here legally, so let me just say: it's a pig.

jokes.text: Andy, I just wanted to say, I am so, so, so... filled... with emotions. As any person would.
jokes.setup: Leslie tries to apologize while avoiding legal liability
jokes.punchline: Her robotic, legally-constrained attempt at human emotion
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy - Leslie's inhuman attempt to express normal emotions within legal constraints

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I want to apologize, but my lawyers have instructed me to say the following: I regret any and all incidents that may or may not have occurred, depending on your definition of 'incident' and 'occurred.' I in no way admit fault, but I do acknowledge your feelings, which are valid, even though I'm not responsible for them.

jokes.text: You were in the pit. We're not conceding that point. You were in a place... we're both here now.
jokes.setup: Leslie tries to state basic facts about the accident
jokes.punchline: Even acknowledging Andy was in the pit is legally problematic
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the legal absurdity - can't even acknowledge basic spatial facts

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Even acknowledging Andy was in the pit is legally problematic.

jokes.text: If you wanna meet, just put a white chalk 'x' on the mailbox across the street from city hall. Or call me back... just call me back.
jokes.setup: Leslie leaves voicemail trying to arrange secret meeting
jokes.punchline: Elaborate spy tactics immediately abandoned for simple phone call
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Leslie's tendency to overcomplicate then immediately simplify

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Hi, it's Leslie. I need to meet with you. It's urgent and confidential. Meet me at the old warehouse on the edge of town at midnight. Come alone. Don't tell anyone. This is very serious.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Actually, you know what, just call me back. My number is 555-0147.

jokes.text: Hey, Andy, it's your aunt. Your mom or dad's sister. I don't know how to tell you this, but... your uncle has passed. He's with Jesus now.
jokes.setup: Leslie pretends to be Andy's aunt in voicemail
jokes.punchline: Awkward fake death announcement to get his attention
jokes.explanation: Decent cringe moment - Leslie's inappropriate fake family emergency

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Hi Andy, this is your Aunt Doris. I'm calling to let you know that your mother has passed away. She died this morning. We're all very sad. Call me back.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: What?! Mom?!

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Just kidding! It's Leslie. But seriously, you need to call me back.

jokes.text: Leslie, the man lived in a pit. He couldn't find a place to live on the earth's surface, so he went under the ground. You're dealing with a grown man who thinks like a gofer.
jokes.setup: Mark explains Andy's situation to Leslie
jokes.punchline: Compares Andy to a burrowing animal
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor - comparing Andy's housing choices to animal behavior

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: Andy's like a badger. He burrows into things, makes a mess, and then disappears for a while.

jokes.text: Thank you so... Why are you naked? Because of what you said, we're getting back together.
jokes.setup: Ann comes to talk to Andy at his place
jokes.punchline: Andy is naked because he misinterpreted her call
jokes.explanation: Standard physical comedy - Andy's literal interpretation leading to inappropriate nudity

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Andy, I need to talk to you about something important.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Oh hey! Yeah, I got your message. You said you were coming over and you wanted me to be ready.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: What? No, I just said I was stopping by—

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I'm ready! I'm so ready!

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Andy, why are you naked?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: You said to be ready! I thought you meant... you know... ready ready.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Oh my god. Put some clothes on.

jokes.text: You should've heard the sexy stuff she said. I said 'hey, Andy, it's Ann, can we talk at my house?' That's not how you said it, you were like, 'Hey, Andy, it's Ann, can you talk at my house?'
jokes.setup: Andy claims Ann said something sexy
jokes.punchline: His 'sexy' version is identical to what she actually said
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy - Andy's ability to hear romance in completely mundane statements

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Ann said something really sexy to me yesterday.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: I did not.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: You did. You said, 'Andy, can you pick up some milk on your way home?'

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: That's... that's exactly what I said.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: In a sexy voice though.

jokes.text: My God, you're such a baby. Literally. Big naked baby, put clothes on.
jokes.setup: Ann is frustrated with Andy's behavior
jokes.punchline: She literally compares him to a naked baby
jokes.explanation: Basic insult comedy - comparing Andy's maturity to an infant

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Andy, you are literally a naked baby. You have no idea what you're doing, you get distracted by literally anything, and you cry when things don't go your way.

jokes.text: None of your clothes fit me, so I'm putting an apron over the front stuff.
jokes.setup: Andy needs to get dressed
jokes.punchline: His solution is an apron covering only the front
jokes.explanation: Decent visual comedy - Andy's impractical clothing solution

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I don't have time to get dressed. I'll just wear an apron.

dialogue.speaker: Someone
dialogue.line: Andy, that only covers the front.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Exactly. Problem solved.

jokes.text: I want Ann back, and she said she needs a guy with a lot of money. That doesn't sound like Ann. I can't really do her voice.
jokes.setup: Andy explains why Ann said she needs money
jokes.punchline: Admits he can't do impressions, undermining his story
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Andy's honest admission undermines his own fabricated story

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Ann said she needs money because... she's starting a small business selling homemade candles.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That doesn't sound like Ann at all.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Well, she told me in her Ann voice.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You can't do impressions, Andy.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I know.

jokes.text: $100,000, I can probably get a bank account.
jokes.setup: Andy explains his financial plan
jokes.punchline: He thinks you need $100,000 to open a bank account
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Andy's complete misunderstanding of basic banking

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: You need like $100,000 to open a bank account.

jokes.text: I can make six bucks a day playing guitar in the street. I can't make six bucks a day.
jokes.setup: Andy considers busking as income
jokes.punchline: Immediately admits he's not good enough to earn even that
jokes.explanation: Good self-awareness joke - Andy recognizes his own limitations honestly

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: You know what? I could totally be a busker. People throw money at street performers all the time.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Andy, you can't even play an instrument.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I could sing!

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Yeah, okay, fair point. I'd probably make like... two dollars a day. And one of those would be pity money.

jokes.text: A guaranteed starting spot on the Indianapolis Colts... inside linebacker. We can't accomplish that.
jokes.setup: Andy lists his settlement demands
jokes.punchline: Wants to play professional football with no qualifications
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Andy's completely unrealistic career aspirations

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I want to play professional football with no qualifications.

jokes.text: I've already been injured in that thing twice. And I can fall in ten more times by 'eventually'.
jokes.setup: Andy explains why the pit needs to be filled soon
jokes.punchline: He realistically predicts he'll fall in again multiple times
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Andy's self-aware prediction of future accidents

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Look, we need to fill that pit soon. Because let's be honest, I'm gonna fall in there at least three more times before it's actually filled.

jokes.text: Usually in these situations a person says 'pinch me.'
jokes.setup: Leslie is amazed the pit is actually being filled
jokes.punchline: She literally asks to be pinched instead of saying the phrase
jokes.explanation: Basic literalism joke - Leslie takes common expression literally

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I can't believe the pit is actually being filled in. This is the best day of my life.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Pinch me. Literally pinch me, because if this is a dream, I don't want to wake up.

jokes.text: Who knows why that gopher does what he does?
jokes.setup: Mark comments on Andy's decision to drop the lawsuit
jokes.punchline: Calls back to earlier gopher comparison
jokes.explanation: Decent callback to earlier animal comparison

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: You know, Andy, dropping that lawsuit is the smartest thing you've done. You're like a gopher.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: A gopher?

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: Yeah. You dig yourself into a hole, then you realize it's a bad idea, and you just... stop digging.

jokes.text: I wish there was something physical that can make me feel this way.
jokes.setup: Leslie describes the physical sensations from taking risks
jokes.punchline: Innocent comment sounds sexual due to phrasing
jokes.explanation: Weak innuendo - relies on accidental sexual interpretation without much craft

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: When you take a risk, your heart races, your palms get sweaty, there's this tingling sensation all over your body.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: That's... that's what you just described.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I know! It's incredible. I'm getting all tingly just thinking about it.

jokes.text: Kaboom! Is a word I made up. It's not in any dictionary. I trick people into building playgrounds in empty lots in their neighborhood. It's an elaborate prank.
jokes.setup: Keith reveals the truth about Kaboom organization
jokes.punchline: The wholesome charity was actually an elaborate prank
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection - completely reframes the entire episode's premise as an elaborate joke

dialogue.speaker: Keith
dialogue.line: The wholesome charity was actually an elaborate prank.

jokes.text: For my next prank, I'm gonna build a hospital in a poor part of China. They'll never see it coming!
jokes.setup: Keith explains his prank philosophy
jokes.punchline: His next 'prank' is building needed infrastructure
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation - takes the prank concept to absurd heights where 'pranks' are actually beneficial public works

dialogue.speaker: Keith
dialogue.line: My prank philosophy is simple: I identify something that will cause maximum chaos and confusion, then I execute it flawlessly.

dialogue.speaker: Keith
dialogue.line: For my next prank, I'm going to build a community center in the South Side. They've needed one for years.

tmdb_overview: Leslie, Ann and the rest of the Parks Department volunteer to help the organization KaBOOM! build a playground for a neighboring town. After building an entire playground in one day, Leslie gets frustrated at the lack of progress with the pit, and takes some advice from Mark to cut through the red tape.
insight: Early Season 2 stumbles with 59 jokes at moderate density but minimal standout moments.
